  • Hide All Code

UK Gender Pay Gap 2025 — Exploratory Data Analysis

Your Name

2026-04-15

1. Introduction

This report presents an exploratory data analysis (EDA) of the UK Gender Pay Gap (GPG) using data from the Annual Survey of Hours and Earnings (ASHE), published by the Office for National Statistics (ONS) in 2025.

The gender pay gap is defined as the difference between men’s and women’s mean or median hourly pay, expressed as a percentage of men’s pay:

\[GPG = \frac{\text{Men's pay} - \text{Women's pay}}{\text{Men's pay}} \times 100\]

A positive value indicates men earn more than women. A negative value indicates women earn more than men.

The analysis examines the GPG across five dimensions:

  • Employment type (all employees, full-time, part-time)
  • Age group
  • Industry sector (SIC 2007)
  • Occupation group (SOC 2020)
  • UK region and nation
  • Public vs private sector

2. Data & Methodology

2.1 Data source

The dataset is drawn from 25 ONS ASHE 2025 tables, each covering a different analytical dimension. The data is aggregated at the group level — it does not contain individual employee records. Each table provides median and mean hourly GPG figures, with separate sheets for all employees, full-time employees, and part-time employees.

Cells marked x in the source files indicate suppressed estimates where the ONS deemed the sample size insufficient for reliable publication. These were treated as missing values (NA) throughout the analysis.

2.2 Data loading & cleaning

library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(scales)
library(patchwork)

setwd(dirname(rstudioapi::getActiveDocumentContext()$path))
source("DataLoading.R")
source("DataCleaning.R")
## Rows: 24
## Columns: 5
## $ description <ord> All employees, 16-17, 18-21, 22-29, 30-39, 40-49, 50-59, 6…
## $ code        <chr> NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA…
## $ gpg_median  <dbl> 12.8, 0.0, 0.5, 3.0, 8.8, 16.2, 19.7, 17.7, 6.9, -1.1, 1.3…
## $ gpg_mean    <dbl> 13.4, -0.7, 4.0, 4.6, 9.2, 14.3, 20.4, 18.4, 10.9, -5.2, 3…
## $ emp_type    <chr> "All", "All", "All", "All", "All", "All", "All", "All", "F…

##           0          24           0           0           0 
## NAs in df_industry_clean:

##           0           0           0           0           0
# Summary of the cleaned datasets
tribble(
  ~Dataset,         ~Rows, ~Dimensions,                      ~NAs,
  "df_age_clean",        nrow(df_age_clean),        "8 age groups × 3 emp types",   sum(is.na(df_age_clean$gpg_median)),
  "df_industry_clean",   nrow(df_industry_clean),   "19 industries × 3 emp types",  sum(is.na(df_industry_clean$gpg_median)),
  "df_occupation_clean", nrow(df_occupation_clean), "9 occupations × 3 emp types",  sum(is.na(df_occupation_clean$gpg_median)),
  "df_pubpriv_clean",    nrow(df_pubpriv_clean),    "3 sectors × 3 emp types",       sum(is.na(df_pubpriv_clean$gpg_median)),
  "df_region_clean",     nrow(df_region_clean),     "12 regions × 3 emp types",      sum(is.na(df_region_clean$gpg_median))
) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Table 1: Summary of cleaned datasets")
Table 1: Summary of cleaned datasets
Dataset Rows Dimensions NAs
df_age_clean 24 8 age groups × 3 emp types 0
df_industry_clean 57 19 industries × 3 emp types 2
df_occupation_clean 27 9 occupations × 3 emp types 0
df_pubpriv_clean 9 3 sectors × 3 emp types 0
df_region_clean 36 12 regions × 3 emp types 0

3. Headline Findings

3.1 Overall gender pay gap

overall <- df_age_clean %>%
  filter(emp_type == "All", description == "All employees")

ft <- df_age_clean %>%
  filter(emp_type == "Full-Time", description == "All employees")

pt <- df_age_clean %>%
  filter(emp_type == "Part-Time", description == "All employees")

The overall UK median GPG in 2025 is 12.8% (mean: 13.4%).

For full-time employees the median gap narrows to 6.9%, while for part-time employees it is -2.9% — a negative figure, meaning women in part-time roles earn more than men on the median.

# Shared colour palette
COL_MEDIAN <- "#378ADD"
COL_MEAN   <- "#D85A30"
COL_POS    <- "#E24B4A"
COL_NEG    <- "#1D9E75"
COL_ALL    <- "#378ADD"
COL_FT     <- "#1D9E75"
COL_PT     <- "#BA7517"

theme_gpg <- function() {
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13) +
    theme(
      plot.title       = element_text(size = 14, face = "bold",
                                      margin = margin(b = 6)),
      plot.subtitle    = element_text(size = 11, colour = "grey40",
                                      margin = margin(b = 12)),
      plot.caption     = element_text(size = 9,  colour = "grey50",
                                      margin = margin(t = 10)),
      axis.title       = element_text(size = 11, colour = "grey30"),
      panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
      panel.grid.major = element_line(colour = "grey92"),
      legend.position  = "bottom",
      legend.title     = element_blank()
    )
}

p1_data <- df_age_clean %>%
  filter(description == "All employees") %>%
  select(emp_type, gpg_median, gpg_mean) %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(gpg_median, gpg_mean),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(
    metric   = recode(metric,
                      gpg_median = "Median GPG",
                      gpg_mean   = "Mean GPG"),
    emp_type = factor(emp_type,
                      levels = c("All", "Full-Time", "Part-Time"))
  )

ggplot(p1_data, aes(x = emp_type, y = value, fill = metric)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(value, 1), "%"),
                vjust = ifelse(value >= 0, -0.4, 1.3)),
            position = position_dodge(width = 0.6), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Median GPG" = COL_MEDIAN,
                               "Mean GPG"   = COL_MEAN)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    title    = "UK gender pay gap by employment type, 2025",
    subtitle = "Positive = men earn more; negative = women earn more",
    x        = "Employment type",
    y        = "Gender pay gap (%)",
    caption  = "Source: ONS ASHE 2025"
  ) +
  theme_gpg()

Figure 1: Overall GPG by employment type

Key finding: The overall gap (12.8% median) is nearly double the full-time gap (6.9%), suggesting the gap is substantially driven by the concentration of women in part-time roles rather than pay inequality within comparable roles.

4. GPG by Age Group

p2_data <- df_age_clean %>%
  filter(emp_type == "All", description != "All employees")

ggplot(p2_data, aes(x = description, y = gpg_median, fill = gpg_median)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(gpg_median, 1), "%"),
                vjust = -0.4), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = COL_NEG, mid = "grey90",
                       high = COL_POS, midpoint = 0) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                     limits = c(-2, 23)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Gender pay gap by age group, 2025 (all employees, median)",
    subtitle = "The gap rises steeply from the 30s — consistent with the motherhood penalty",
    x        = "Age group",
    y        = "Median GPG (%)",
    caption  = "Source: ONS ASHE 2025"
  ) +
  theme_gpg()

Figure 2: Median GPG by age group 

df_age_clean %>%
  filter(description != "All employees") %>%
  mutate(emp_type = factor(emp_type,
                           levels = c("All", "Full-Time", "Part-Time"))) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = description, y = gpg_median,
             colour = emp_type, group = emp_type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("All"       = COL_ALL,
                                 "Full-Time" = COL_FT,
                                 "Part-Time" = COL_PT)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  labs(
    title    = "GPG by age group and employment type, 2025",
    subtitle = "Part-time workers show a structurally different pattern to full-time employees",
    x        = "Age group",
    y        = "Median GPG (%)",
    caption  = "Source: ONS ASHE 2025"
  ) +
  theme_gpg()

Figure 3: GPG by age group and employment type

Key finding: The gap is near-zero for workers aged 16–29, then rises sharply between the 22–29 and 40–49 brackets.

5. GPG by Industry

p4_data <- df_industry_clean %>%
  filter(emp_type == "All") %>%
  arrange(gpg_median) %>%
  mutate(short_label = factor(short_label, levels = short_label))

ggplot(p4_data, aes(x = short_label, y = gpg_median, fill = gpg_median)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 12.8, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = COL_POS, linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = 13.8,
           label = "UK average (12.8%)",
           colour = COL_POS, size = 3.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(gpg_median, 1), "%"),
                hjust = ifelse(gpg_median >= 0, -0.15, 1.15)),
            size = 3.2) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = COL_NEG, mid = "grey90",
                       high = COL_POS, midpoint = 0) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                     limits = c(-5, 32)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Gender pay gap by industry, 2025 (all employees, median)",
    subtitle = "Financial & insurance activities has the largest gap — more than double the UK average",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Median GPG (%)",
    caption  = "Source: ONS ASHE 2025"
  ) +
  theme_gpg()

Figure 4: Median GPG by industry 

df_industry_clean %>%
  filter(emp_type == "All") %>%
  arrange(desc(gpg_median)) %>%
  select(Industry = short_label,
         `Median GPG (%)` = gpg_median,
         `Mean GPG (%)` = gpg_mean) %>%
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(., 1))) %>%
  knitr::kable(caption = "Table 2: GPG by industry, all employees (sorted by median GPG)")
Table 2: GPG by industry, all employees (sorted by median GPG)
Industry Median GPG (%) Mean GPG (%)
Finance & insurance 27.2 27.9
Mining 23.1 13.9
Prof, sci & tech 18.6 16.5
Construction 17.1 11.5
Education 17.0 12.1
Manufacturing 14.8 8.1
Info & comms 13.6 10.6
Other services 13.1 11.1
Health & social work 12.8 19.3
Electricity & gas 12.1 11.5
Real estate 11.7 14.2
Wholesale & retail 10.5 12.0
Public admin 8.5 4.0
Admin & support 7.2 10.0
Arts & entertainment 5.7 26.5
Transport 4.2 6.7
Agriculture 3.4 2.8
Accommodation & food 3.0 4.8
Water & sewerage -1.5 -7.5

Key finding: Financial & insurance activities (27.2%) has a gap more than double the national average, likely driven by a highly male-dominated senior workforce. Water & sewerage (−1.5%) is the only sector where women out-earn men on the median.

6. GPG by Occupation

p5_data <- df_occupation_clean %>%
  filter(emp_type == "All") %>%
  arrange(gpg_median) %>%
  mutate(description = factor(description, levels = description))

ggplot(p5_data, aes(x = description, y = gpg_median, fill = gpg_median)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 12.8, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = COL_POS, linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = 13.8,
           label = "UK average (12.8%)",
           colour = COL_POS, size = 3.2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(gpg_median, 1), "%"),
                hjust = ifelse(gpg_median >= 0, -0.15, 1.15)),
            size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = COL_NEG, mid = "grey90",
                       high = COL_POS, midpoint = 0) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%"),
                     limits = c(0, 20)) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Gender pay gap by occupation group, 2025 (all employees, median)",
    subtitle = "Skilled trades and associate professionals show the largest within-occupation gaps",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Median GPG (%)",
    caption  = "Source: ONS ASHE 2025"
  ) +
  theme_gpg()

Figure 5: Median GPG by occupation group

7. GPG by Region

df_region_clean %>%
  filter(emp_type == "All") %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(gpg_median, gpg_mean),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(metric = recode(metric,
                         gpg_median = "Median GPG",
                         gpg_mean   = "Mean GPG")) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = description, y = value, fill = metric)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 12.8, linetype = "dotted",
             colour = "grey40", linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Median GPG" = COL_MEDIAN,
                               "Mean GPG"   = COL_MEAN)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Gender pay gap by UK region, 2025 (all employees)",
    subtitle = "London's mean gap (17.8%) far exceeds its median (11.8%) — a sign of high-earner skew",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "GPG (%)",
    caption  = "Source: ONS ASHE 2025"
  ) +
  theme_gpg()

Figure 6: GPG by UK region — median vs mean

Key finding: London shows the largest divergence between median (11.8%) and mean (17.8%) GPG of any region. This gap between the two metrics signals a right-skewed pay distribution — a small number of very high-earning men pull the mean upward without affecting the median.

8. Public vs Private Sector

df_pubpriv_clean %>%
  pivot_longer(cols = c(gpg_median, gpg_mean),
               names_to = "metric", values_to = "value") %>%
  mutate(
    metric   = recode(metric,
                      gpg_median = "Median GPG",
                      gpg_mean   = "Mean GPG"),
    emp_type = factor(emp_type,
                      levels = c("All", "Full-Time", "Part-Time"))
  ) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = description, y = value, fill = metric)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.65) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Median GPG" = COL_MEDIAN,
                               "Mean GPG"   = COL_MEAN)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = function(x) paste0(x, "%")) +
  facet_wrap(~ emp_type, ncol = 3) +
  labs(
    title    = "Gender pay gap by sector and employment type, 2025",
    subtitle = "Private sector shows a consistently wider gap across all employment types",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "GPG (%)",
    caption  = "Source: ONS ASHE 2025"
  ) +
  theme_gpg() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 20, hjust = 1))

Figure 7: GPG by sector and employment type
